summary: Fowler argues that Model Driven Architecture (MDA) is unlikely to succeed, comparing it unfavorably to the failed CASE tools of the 1980s. While CASE tools promised to revolutionize software development through graphical modeling, they failed because they couldn't provide a coherent programming environment more effective than alternatives. MDA faces the same fundamental problem: UML, despite efforts to make it computationally complete, doesn't offer a more effective development environment than writing code in modern languages. Fowler notes that even if UML became an effective programming environment, it would still need to achieve mainstream adoption, which isn't guaranteed. He dismisses secondary arguments about standards, platform independence, and the superiority of pictures over text.
key_points: MDA repeats the same promises that CASE tools made in the 1980s, which ultimately failed to deliver
UML works well for sketching design ideas but lacks the formality and cohesion needed to be a complete programming solution
Drawing sequence diagrams or activity diagrams is not as effective as writing code in a modern language
Pictures are not inherently better than text for programming - flow charts versus pseudocode demonstrates this
Even excellent programming environments don't guarantee mainstream adoption, as Smalltalk's history shows
money_quotes: Some people think that Model Driven Architecture (MDA) will be biggest shift in software development since the move from assembler to the first high level languages. Others think that it's nothing more than Night of the Living Case Tools.
I can't see that drawing sequence diagrams or activity diagrams is as good, let alone better, than writing code in a modern language.
As an ex-Smalltalker I know only too well that even the best languages don't always make it into the mainstream.
Much of the boosting of UML seems to be based on the statement that pictures are better than text. In cases this is true, but I see no evidence for that in general.
I would really like to see software development raised by a level of abstraction. But I don't see that the UML provides this abstraction jump - and without it the MDA will not succeed.
Generating pattern implementations is missing at least half the point of patterns.
key_insight: For a new development paradigm to succeed, it must provide a more effective programming environment than existing alternatives - theoretical completeness and industry standards are insufficient without practical superiority.
